channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Contradiction
item.description:
In formal logic, contradiction typically means that two claims cannot be true together. In dialectical analysis, contradiction also names a structured tension internal to a form, where the conditions of that form’s reproduction generate forces that destabilize it.

Dialectical contradiction is not a permission to ignore rigor. It is a methodological signal: identify how incompatibility is produced, mediated, and historically transformed.

Related terms

  • Dialectics
  • Mediation
  • Historicization

item.title: Determinate Negation
item.description:
Determinate negation is negation with content. A form is not merely rejected; it is transformed according to its own internal limits, producing a successor form that preserves certain determinations while reconfiguring their role [@hegel-dialectics-sep-2019].

This distinguishes determinate negation from abstract negation:

  • Abstract negation: simple opposition (not-X).
  • Determinate negation: structured transition (X becomes re-specified through its contradiction).

The concept explains how dialectical development can involve real rupture without becoming arbitrary. What follows is constrained by what is negated.

item.title: Historicization
item.description:
Historicization is the method of treating an object as historically produced rather than timelessly given.

To historicize a concept is to ask when it emerged, through which conflicts or practices it stabilized, and how its meaning shifts across periods. To historicize an institution is to track the sequence of forces, decisions, and constraints through which it was formed and reorganized.

In dialectical work, historicization prevents abstraction from freezing social forms into essences. It keeps analysis tied to determinate conditions and temporal development.

item.title: Immanent Critique
item.description:
Immanent critique is criticism from within: it evaluates practices, institutions, or theories using norms they themselves claim to uphold. In critical theory, this method is central because it links diagnosis to historically situated contradictions rather than external ideal models [@critical-theory-sep-2023].

Immanent critique differs from merely internal critique. It is not only consistency checking; it is transformative analysis of crisis-tendencies within a form of life.

Related terms

  • Immanence
  • Contradiction
  • Historicization

item.title: Mediation
item.description:
Mediation is the principle that determinations do not stand alone or move in isolation. They are connected through intermediate relations, processes, and forms.

In dialectics, mediation opposes immediacy. An immediate account says A produces B directly. A mediated account asks which structures, temporal phases, and relation chains connect A to B.

In social analysis, mediation helps distinguish structural explanation from narrative sequence. Saying “crisis produced reform” is incomplete unless the institutions, conflicts, and mechanisms that carried the transition are specified.

item.title: Nonidentity
item.description:
Nonidentity names the mismatch between concept and object: objects are never exhausted by the concepts that classify them. In Adorno’s negative dialectics, this is not a minor epistemic inconvenience but a structural condition for critique [@adorno-sep-2025].

Nonidentity blocks premature reconciliation. It keeps thought attentive to what identity-thinking suppresses: particularity, remainder, and the historical violence embedded in forced conceptual sameness.

Related terms

  • Dialectics
  • Reification
  • Totality

item.title: Principal Contradiction
item.description:
Principal contradiction identifies which contradiction is decisive in a conjuncture when multiple contradictions coexist. In Mao’s formulation, this role can shift as conditions change; principal status is historical, not eternal [@mao-on-contradiction-1937].

This concept is strategic: it orders analytic and political priority.

Related terms

  • Secondary Contradiction
  • Contradiction
  • Historicization

item.title: Reification
item.description:
Reification is the transformation of historically produced social relations into apparently natural or thing-like facts.

In Marxist and Lukácsian analysis, reification obscures mediation: what is socially produced appears as if it were simply given, autonomous, and beyond alteration [@lukacs-sep-2023].

The critical task is de-reification: recovering the processes, relations, and institutions that produce the appearance of objectivity.

Related terms

  • Totality
  • Dialectics
  • Historicization

item.title: Secondary Contradiction
item.description:
Secondary contradictions are contradictions within a process that do not occupy the decisive position at a given stage. They can intensify, recede, and under changed conditions become principal [@mao-on-contradiction-1937].

Treating secondary contradictions as irrelevant is an error; the concept marks subordination, not insignificance.

Related terms

  • Principal Contradiction
  • Contradiction

item.title: Sublation
item.description:
Sublation (German: Aufhebung) names the dialectical movement in which something is negated without being simply destroyed. A prior form is cancelled as self-sufficient, preserved in transformed form, and raised into a richer determination [@hegel-dialectics-sep-2019].

Sublation is a key reason dialectical development is neither pure continuity nor pure rupture.

Related terms

  • Determinate Negation
  • Mediation
  • Dialectics

item.title: Totality
item.description:
Totality is the methodological demand to analyze parts through the structured whole that conditions their meaning and function.

In dialectical usage, totality does not mean a closed or final system. It means that phenomena are relationally constituted: labor, law, state, and culture are not self-explanatory in isolation.

In Lukácsian terms, totality is central to overcoming fragmented or reified appearances in social analysis [@lukacs-sep-2023].

Related terms

  • Dialectics
  • Mediation
  • Historicization

item.title: Uneven and Combined Development
item.description:
Uneven and combined development names a historical pattern in which different developmental tempos coexist and interpenetrate, producing hybrid social forms rather than linear stage succession [@trotsky-hrr-1930].

In contemporary theory, it functions as a general historical-dialectical category for relational development under asymmetry [@rosenberg-ucd-2013].

Related terms

  • Historicization
  • Totality
  • History of Dialectics

item.title: Dialectics
item.description: Dialectics is a method of reasoning through contradiction. In its most familiar form — thesis, antithesis, synthesis — it proposes that understanding advances when a position (thesis) encounters its negation (antithesis) and both are resolved into a higher unity (synthesis) that preserves and transcends both. The method has a long history, from Plato’s Socratic dialogues through Hegel’s philosophy of spirit to Marx’s materialist inversion, and it remains one of the dominant intellectual methods in critical theory, political analysis, and journalism.
